attributes.stem:
A previously well 23-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 4-day history of fever followed by two episodes of seizures.

On examination she is pale with petechial rashes on both arms and her Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) is 10. The rest of the physical examination is unremarkable.

Her laboratory investigations show:

  • Haemoglobin 78 gm/dl (111-145)

  • WBC 9 x10^9/L (4-10)

  • Platelets 25 x10^9/L (150-400)

  • PT 10 seconds (9.1-13.1)

  • aPTT 30 seconds (22.9-34.5)

  • Cr 150 μmol/L (45-90)

  • LDH 4890 U/L (140-280)

Her peripheral blood film is shown (Image):
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would be expected to be seen on her blood film?
attributes.explanation:
This is a case of thrombotic thrombocytopenia purpura (TTP), which is a rare but life threatening cause of microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (MAHA).

TTP is distinguished from other MAHA by severe deficiency of ADAMTS13 (a disintegrin and metalloproteinase with a thrombospondin type 1 motif, member 13), a plasma protein that cleaves von Willebrand factor (vWF) multimers. Without the proteolytic activity of ADAMTS13, the uncleaved ultra-large vWF multimers accumulate and induce excessive platelet adhesion and aggregation, leading to formation of disseminated platelet-rich thrombi in microvasculature. The large vWF multimers also attach to activated platelets and mediate further platelet aggregation. Platelets end up being removed and the resulting fibrin strand formation remains. These fibrin strands along with the stress from the blood flow cause fragmentation of the red blood cells, leading to schistocyte formation.

TTP is characterised by haemolytic anaemia, presence of red blood cell fragments or schistocytes on the peripheral blood smear (shown in the image), thrombocytopenia, fever, neurological symptoms and renal dysfunction.

The first laboratory clue towards TTP is the presence of red cell fragments/schistocytes on peripheral blood film. In patients with clinical suspicion of TTP or MAHA, review of peripheral smear is very important.

Diagnosis of TTP is challenging because of its diverse clinical manifestations and overlap in clinical presentation with other causes of MAHAs [like complement-mediated haemolytic uraemic syndrome (HUS), Shiga toxin-associated HUS, disseminated intravascular coagulopathy (DIC), drug-induced TMA, etc.]. An ADAMTS13 activity level of less than 10% supports the diagnosis of TTP in appropriate clinical contexts.

The other red cell morphology namely, blister cells are seen in haemolytic anemia due to G6PD enzyme deficiency, burr cells are seen in renal failure, target cells and tear-drop cells are commonly observed in patients with haemoglobinopathies or liver dysfunction and bone marrow infiltration respectively.
learningPoints: Thrombotic thrombocytopenia purpura (TTP) is characterised by the presence of red cell fragments/schistocytes on peripheral blood smear.
references: Zheng XL, Vesely SK, Cataland SR, Coppo P, Geldziler B, Iorio A, et al. ISTH guidelines for the diagnosis of thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura. J Thromb Haemost. 2020 Oct;18(10):2486-2495

Fox LC, Cohney SJ, Kausman JY, Shortt J, Hughes PD, Wood EM, Isbel NM, et al. Consensus opinion on diagnosis and management of thrombotic microangiopathy in Australia and New Zealand. Nephrology (Carlton). 2018 Jun;23(6):507-517
